sentences.text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]
I was born in Lithuania in 1930.
And Lithuania, at that time, was independent.
We lived in the second largest city called Kovna, or Kaunas, as it is known now.
And I come from a middle-class Jewish family, which was not especially religious.
And in fact, I am of the few Lithuanian Jews who spoke neither Hebrew nor Yiddish.
We spoke Russian at home.
My father was an automobile dealer.
He sold Willys-Overland cars that no longer exists.
He sold Harley-Davidson motorcycles, which do exist.
And from time to time, we would get an American representative to come to the provinces to see us.
And I became probably the only kid in Lithuania who had a Mickey Mouse toothbrush, which must have been very important because I still remember that.
My father's name was Nicholas.
We had just myself.
I had an older brother from my father's first wife, but he had emigrated to Israel in 1936, long before it was fashionable.
And it caused quite a scandal in the family because he had an engineering degree and was a very bright young man.
And instead, he went to Palestine to work as a-- I think, on the dock in Haifa at night.
And nobody could understand why he did that.
My childhood was very normal.
I went to school.
As a matter of fact, I didn't go to Jewish school.
I went to a Catholic school, Lithuanian.
Me and another kid were the only two Jews in my particular class.
And whenever they had religion, we would go and stand in the hallway while everybody else was taught Christianity.
I went to school.
I liked some of my teachers.
I didn't like some of the others.
I was sure they gave us too much homework.
I bugged my parents when they wouldn't let me go see the movies that I wanted to see.
I was crazy about Shirley Temple.
I was sure I'd grow up and marry her.
I didn't.
And a perfectly normal childhood.
It's-- I lived, nice father, nice mother, nice little dog named Tommy, a wire hair terrier.
I didn't know that very abnormal times are going to come around and finish this very normal existence.
Some, but not a hell of a lot.
One of the reasons probably being that I had blue eyes and blond hair.
I spoke perfect Lithuanian without an accent.
I did not look like a Jew.
I did not act like a Jew.
So in the street, nobody especially bothered me.
Most of my friends were Jewish, and we had this little group that we hung out together.
And from time to time, there would be some people who would make a remark or some kid would make a remark, and something about why don't you all go back to Palestine.
And I would go back to my parents and say, what is this about Palestine?
But in general, no, it wasn't all that bad.
Did your mother have an occupation?
No.
She was more or less-- I hesitate to say it, but I suppose it's true-- like a society lady.
There were a lot of parties, and I had a nanny that took care of me-- not a Jewish woman-- whom I love dearly.
And she was a housewife.
She was my father's wife.
I don't know that my family noticed it all that much.
In 1936, I think it was-- '37, maybe '38-- my brother came back from Palestine very briefly.
And he told me-- he told my father a story, which my father told me later when it was much too late.
My brother told my father a story that has to do with how do you catch monkeys in Africa?
And he said, you catch monkeys in Africa by putting a large cage in the jungle clearing, and inside the cage you hang a banana on a string.
And a monkey comes by and grabs a hold of the banana, but he can't pull it out because his fist with the banana won't go through the cages-- through the bars.
But he doesn't want to let go either.
So he sits there holding onto the banana.
And my brother said to my father, let go of the banana.
Leave while you can still leave.
And my father said, don't be ridiculous.
Lithuania is a small, independent country.
Who needs us?
Hitler doesn't want us.
Stalin doesn't want us.
Everything will be all right.
Forget it.
And later on in the ghetto, when it was much too late, he told me the story about the banana, which I never forgot.
So when did actually the trouble begin?
Well, the war began in 1939, as you know, in September.
This is when Hitler and Stalin attacked Poland and divided it.
Lithuania, at that point, was not affected.
But Hitler and Stalin had signed a-- an accord dividing the world between them for after the war.
And Lithuania, as well as Latvia and Estonia and, by the way, Finland, also were supposed to go to Stalin.
So shortly after that, in 1940 the Soviet Army marched in and took over Lithuania.
Lithuania was no longer independent.
My father's business was taken away.
I had to go to a different school.
We changed apartments.
But other than that, nothing much was affected in my personal life.
My father got a job.
And the one thing that did happen is that Russia and Germany were allies at the time.
So naturally, nothing bad could be said about Hitler in the Soviet press or on the Soviet radio.
So we really had no idea what the Germans were doing to the Jews in occupied Poland.
We knew that Hitler was an anti-Semite.
But the feeling was it's a civilized nation.
It's an educated nation.
How bad can it be?
And so nobody really worried all that much.

The main worry was, at that particular time towards-- after-- during the first year of Soviet occupation, is that the Soviets were deporting many, many people to the Soviet Union-- all of the intellectuals, all of the businessmen, all of the people who could have been some kind of a resistance.
They deported something like 100,000 Lithuanian citizens, of whom about 25% were Jews.
Totally disproportionate because only about less than 10% of Lithuania's population were Jewish.
And my parents were very much afraid that they, too, would be deported.

And something happened at that time, which I think left an imprint on my life for many, many years to come, namely that a person should basically try to be a good person, even if you don't see much sense in it.
What happened was, I had gone to a summer camp.
I joined the Communist Youth movement, the Young Pioneers, and they had summer camps.
And I went to a summer camp, which was right on the shore of the Baltic.
And it was  in the border zone, right next to the border with Germany.
I didn't like the camp.
It was cold, and it was rainy, and the kids were nasty.
In fact, there was anti-Semitism.
And then suddenly I received a phone call at the camp, and it was my father.
And he told me my mother was sick, and would I come home immediately?
I went to the camp director and I said, my mother is sick.
He said, no, forget it.
You've got another week or so to go.
You'll go when you're all done.
In the meantime, if you can get us a doctor certificate, then we'll let you out.
This way, how do we really know?
Go back to your bunk and forget about it.
So I left, and I was worried.
Oh, yeah.
And then I tried to call my father from the camp to try and get a doctor's certificate, and I was told no calls are being put through.
This was a border zone.
You cannot call out.
So the next day, I took my little suitcase in the morning, and I walked to the railroad station.
I was 10 years old.
And I tried to buy a ticket to go back to Lithuania, to Kaunas, even though they said I couldn't, and the cashier wouldn't sell it to me.
Then the Russian Army patrol came by, and the lieutenant in charge was impressed with the way I spoke Russian.
He told the cashier to sell me a ticket.
I went back.
And the first thing I realized was my mother wasn't sick, and I was very surprised.
And it turns out that they started deporting people.
My parents were very sure that they would be in the next bunch of deportees.
They were very much afraid that I'd stay behind.
So my father tried to put through a long-distance phone call, and he was told, you can't put through phone calls.
It's a border zone.
And he got this long-distance operator who recognized his voice.
And she said, aren't you Mr.
Frumkin?
And he said, yes, I am.
She says, you don't recognize me, but I used to put through long-distance calls for you when you were in business.
And every time you'd go to Paris or Berlin or whatever on business, you would bring me back presents, and I really appreciated that.
You know what?
I can lose my job for this, but I'll put your phone call through.
So she put the phone call through.
My father got through.
I got back.
Three days after I got back, the war broke out.
The Germans occupied that camp within the first 24 hours, and all the Jewish kids were shot.
What was your father's occupation?
And tell me the names of your mother and father, please.
My father's name is Nicholas, Nicholas Frumkin.
My mother's name is Zila Oster, O-S-T-E-R.
And he was an automobile dealer.
What?
I'm sorry what did you--
No, no, no, no, no, no, no.
Three days later the G-- the war began, and the Germans advanced into Lithuania.
And the first thing they took over was the summer camp in which I was, which was right on the border.
But had I stayed in the camp and had my father not been nice to the telephone operator, I wouldn't be there.
I wouldn't be here.
The next thing that happened is that the Soviet Army retreated very quickly.
There was no fighting.
The Soviet Army was completely demoralized.
And there was a three day period in our city before the Germans arrived.

And during that three day period, Lithuanian nationalist partisans were going house to house taking out Jews wherever they could find them and executing them in a central place in the center of the city.
Pictures were taken that exist of that.
And they came to our house, and they took out my father, my mother, and me, and there were about six or seven men with rifles.
They put us up against the wall in the street, and I guess they were going to take us.
And one of the people in that little bunch looked at my father, and he said, aren't you Mr.
Frumkin?
And my father said, yes, I am.
He says, don't you remember me?
I used to work for you.
I was a mechanic at your garage up until about four years ago, he said.
Come on, guys, this is a very nice man.
Let him go.
Let him go.
And they let us back in the house.
And again, you know, that was that.
It was one of those coincidences.
In fact, I think the major coincidence was that we lived where we lived in the city where we lived.
Because only later we found out that as the Germans advanced through Lithuania right after the front-line troops, there came the sonderkommandos, the special units.
And their job was to go into every village and to every town and kill all the Jews that were there, which they did, with the help of Lithuanian civilians who joined them.
And they established ghettos in the three largest cities-- in Vilnius, in Kaunas, and in Siauliai.
But the other two were eliminated before the war was over.
So in our ghetto, we're the only Lithuanian Jews that remained alive, plus those who had gone into the forest to fight plus those who were hidden by Gentiles.
But the numbers, there were about 225,000 Lithuanian Jews before the war.
There were about 18,000, maybe 19,000 that survived.
So again, the coincidence of living where we lived was a very helpful thing.
Anyway, the Germans arrived very shortly-- three days after the war began, within three days.
And they issued regulations about wearing yellow stars, about not walking on sidewalks, the usual stuff.
Don't go to parks.
Don't go to hospitals.
And then they established a ghetto outside of the city in a suburb of the city.
It was a slum.
And we were given two weeks in which the few Lithuanians who lived in the ghetto had to move out, and all the Jews had to move in.
And it was surrounded with barbed wire, and there were guards at the gates and at the fences and so on.
And it was single-family shacks, not really houses.
There was no running water.
There was no indoor plumbing.
Electricity worked two hours in the morning, two hours at night, and extremely crowded.
The ghetto authority that was established by the Germans, the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called, they controlled the living space, and everybody was allocated three square meters.
And they would check it out.
If you had more than that, they would move in more people so that every room had at least one family, and some rooms had, you know, two or three families living there.
As a child, you accommodate yourself.
You sort of accept things as they are.
I realized that this whole thing was abnormal, but pretty soon I fell into the, more or less, routine of the whole situation.
Oh, yeah.
There were.
There were many children at first because they weren't-- they really didn't start picking on children and start exterminating seriously at first.
No.
Not at that point.
Later on, yes.

What happened was that-- well, to begin with, what, what happened almost immediately was that the Germans announced that they needed 2,000 young men, and they needed them with college educations, preferably fluent in two out of three languages-- Russian, Lithuanian, and German.
And that these people would be given a chance to do easy work, research at universities, and so on.
And it sounded absolutely marvelous.
So anybody who had any protection, any pull, tried to get in in those 2,000.
And then the day came, and they marched out, the best and the brightest we had.
And they took them to a place called the Ninth Fort, which was about 10 miles out of the city, an old World War I Russian fortress.
And this is where they were killing people.
They were bringing in people there from Lithuania, but also from Germany and from France and from all over the place.
And it wasn't anything sophisticated, just machine guns and bulldozers and so on.
So they killed the 2,000.
They were never seen again.

Then the other-- the next thing that happened was that they announced that all of the gold, and silver, and valuables, and furs, and radios, watches, clocks, all that, had to be delivered within 72 hours.
And they apparently felt that they weren't getting enough, so they decided to show us that they were serious about it.
And what they did was they sent armed patrols to every corner house on every street.
And out of every corner house, they took two men at random, shot him, and just let him lie there.
And we happened to live on a corner at that time, so they came to our house.
They took out my grandfather and one of my uncles, and we heard shots.
And we came out, and there were the two dead bodies.
And the Germans were gone.
And most people dug up the stuff and turned it in.
So I guess it worked to a certain extent.
We also had sort of a semi-funny situation with my dog because we had my little Tommy was a purebred wire hair terrier from Germany, as a matter of fact.
They'd bought him in Berlin.
And I loved him very much.
But in the ghetto, how can you have a dog?
You know, there wasn't enough food.
So it was kind of a worrisome situation.
And then at one point, oh, maybe three, four weeks after we got into the ghetto, this big German staff car pulls up in front of our house, and I was playing outdoors.
And some German officers pile out, and they're yelling in German, [GERMAN].
Where is the boy?
Where is the boy?
And I ran into the house, and my mother got freaked out.
And she started changing my clothes, putting on different color clothes.
She thought I had done something to the Germans.
They just came in looking for the dog.
I was-- I was almost 11.
Didn't it infuriate you?
Of course it did.
Of course it did.
But I think the dog had a good war.
It's, you know, one of those things.
Look, from the point of view of a kid, it was very boring.
The adults would go to work.
The kids would stay behind.
There was really nothing for us to do.
From time to time, there were Aktions, when they would come and search through the ghetto, and they would take people out.
Or they would surround an area of the ghetto and, you know, sort the people out.
Or they would go through the entire ghetto looking for children or old people.
They came and burned down the hospital that we had a couple of times.
But in between the Aktions, which were totally unpredictable, as a kid, it was really very boring.
You had to find things to do because days were long.
And we played a lot of soccer.
There was-- you know, you would go out on the field and try and pick up a team.
And I'd say, hey, where's Joey?
And somebody would say, oh, Joey, didn't you know?
They took him away last Wednesday.
And I'd say, oh, yeah, what a shame.
He was a good center forward.
Now we have to find somebody else.
We'd go to the houses where the people had been taken away.
Before the-- the Jewish authority, they had carts that would go to the place-- to the houses of the people who had been taken away in the previous Aktion.
And they would take all the belongings.
They would be taken to a large warehouse and sorted out, and it would be distributed among the people who needed it.
But before the Jewish authority could get it, we, the kids, would go to those houses-- to the houses and search through them and look for books to have something to read.
I had established a lending library where kids would come.
I even started the lottery once.
We had this thing there where we played for buttons, for different kinds of military buttons that had different values.
And I told my father that I've established a lottery for people who buy tickets, and you can win a prize of 15 buttons or 20 buttons.
My father got very angry.
He says, if you're doing this, you know that you are going to win.
This is a sure thing.
You are not gambling.
You're cheating.
And he wouldn't let me do that.
My father was a very ethical man.
But this is some of the stuff we did.
I put on-- I and a couple friends-- a friend of mine who played the accordion-- we wrote a couple of little playlets that we put on.
And people would come in and pay us with buttons to come and watch the plays.
So there was that.
But you never knew, you know, when they would come and take you away.
One of the most frightening 24 hours, I think, I spent in my life was when my father and I went to visit some friends in a completely different section of the ghetto from where we lived.
My mother stayed behind at home.
We went to visit.
And these were people-- they had a son about my age, and there was a father and, and a wi-- and a mother and my father and I.
And all of a sudden, there was screaming in the streets.

And when you saw the, the thing that really struck me was when they run into the ghetto, when everybody is running in all directions yelling and screaming, and it's total chaos, and you don't know what is happening.
Well, this is what was happening outside the window of the house we were in.
And this was a one-story shack like all of them, but the entrance to it was sort of through the alley and around the back.
And there was also a front entrance, which was a different part of the house, a different apartment where different people lived.
Somehow or other, they were emptying that section of the ghetto completely.
They were taking everybody out.
They were going from house to house, from apartment to apartment.
They never got to the one where we were.
And all of a sudden it was quiet.
And we could hear the boots of the sentries walking in the street outside the window.
We didn't dare turn on the light.
It was dark.
And there we were, sitting through the night, not knowing if there was anybody left alive in the entire ghetto, whether anybody was ever coming back.
What was happening?
Where did they take the people?
Knowing that if we go out, who knows?
It might be good thing, or they might immediately kill us or what.
We sat there through the night, not daring to move, not daring to talk, not daring to have any food.
It was the longest night of my life more than likely.
And then in the morning, around 10:00 in the morning, all of a sudden there was a great deal of noise.
And we heard footsteps, and some of the people were coming back.
And apparently they had taken everybody out.
They sorted them out.
They took away the old and the-- and the sick and the young, and they let the other people come back.
And as we were-- we still didn't know what was going on.
As the people were coming in, all of a sudden the door flies open.
And a German officer comes running in, who apparently was just checking out to see, you know-- I don't know what he was checking out.
But he found us, not knowing that we were not among those who had never been taken away.
And he started asking questions of the adults, and the adults did not really know what to answer.
They didn't want to be caught.
But it all went off well.
We had no idea whether my mother was still alive, and naturally, we rushed back.
And apparently, it was only that particular section of the ghetto that was affected.
And my mother was going out of her mind, knowing that we had gone to where this thing was taking place.
Eventually, eventually.
But while in the ghetto-- well, the big Aktion took place on October 29, 1942.
This was at a time when we had about 32,000 people left in the ghetto.
We started with around 48,000.
And we were told that everybody had to report to this large field in the center of the ghetto, and anybody found in the houses would be immediately executed.
So all of us showed up, and there was a bunch of Germans sitting at a long table on one side of the ghetto.
And we marched past, and there was one German who would go right, left, left, right, sorting us all out.
And all the people to the right were taken to one side.
And throughout the day, they would bring in pieces of paper showing how many they had.
And when it was 10,000, that was it.
It was already late afternoon.
All the people on the left were told to go home.
The 10,000 were taken to the Ninth Fort and executed.
And here's a peculiar thing.
When-- I got a book at home-- you might have it also-- called the And in the back, it has statistics, and one of the statistics is the so-called Jordan List.
And the Jordan List is the list of how many people were executed at the Ninth Fort at any given day, and I naturally looked up October 29, 1942.
And a very peculiar thing.
There was something like 3,000 something or other Jewish men, and 4,000 something or other Jewish women, and 3,000 something or other Jewish children.
And by the way, it is marked, 'Elimination of superfluous Jews from Ghetto Kaunas.' And the total of all these weird numbers comes out to 9,800, 9,800.
And I looked at it, and I couldn't figure it out.
And 200 people stayed alive that day.
And maybe me, another one of the-- I don't know if he was drunk, or just ignorant, or maybe compassionate.
But this is one of those strange things that happened.
I just wanted to say something about heroism, you know, dealing with, with this strange American idea that suffering is heroism and that all survivors are heroes just because they suffered.
I mean, I think that's a bunch of hooey.
Heroism is not suffering.
Heroism is doing something that you don't have to do, running into a building.
Suffering deserves sympathy and tender loving care.
But just because you were chained to a wall in Beirut for five years doesn't make you a hero because you would much rather be in Chicago.
You don't have a choice.
And I'm not a hero because I would have much rather grown up in New York or Malibu or Paris.
I didn't have that choice.
I'm not a hero.
But there are heroes, and there was heroism.
There were-- there were Jewish policemen in the ghetto, Jewish young men who wore armbands that [NON-ENGLISH].
And they kept order.
They organized the labor brigades and so on.
The Germans knew that there were a lot of hiding places in the ghetto.
And as you saw in there were false ceilings and false walls.
And you could go through an oven into somewhere under the house.
They called them malinas.
Malinas were the hiding places.
And the Germans took the 300 ghetto police to the Ninth Fort.
And they told them, they said, each one of you must betray a malina.
Surely you know where they are.
And those who tell us where they are will be allowed to go back in the ghetto.
Those who don't talk, sorry.
You're not going anywhere.
And they gave them 72 hours.
And after 72 hours, out of the 300 12 came back. 288 died.
Nobody knows their names.
Nobody will ever know their names.
That is heroism, you know, not just surviving or whatever.
Well, what happened then, our numbers kept getting smaller and smaller, and the Russians started fighting back real well.
And as the front came nearer, they decided to evacuate those of us who were left, take us to slave labor to Germany.
And again, we were loaded into cattle cars that I think people are familiar with-- very crowded, no food, no water, a bucket for toilet facilities.
In fact, we stopped at a couple of railroad stations-- I don't know in what German towns-- where kids were out there on the platforms throwing rocks at us in Germany.
But another one of those coincidences happened was when the train crossed the German border.
It stopped, and the doors were flung open, and we heard a German going back and forth yelling, women and children out.
And there weren't that many children left.
But I walked out with my mother, walking along the railroad track.
And I was 13.
And suddenly, for no reason whatsoever, I thought maybe I better stay with my father.
My father was a little older.
He was not all that well.
I said, mom, I'm going to go back on the train.
And she said fine.
She didn't know what was right or wrong.
So I jumped back on the train.
She went on by herself, and which was very smart and very lucky.
Because that very night, all the women who did have children were selected with the children for elimination at Stutthof.
And I lost my grandmother, and I lost two of my aunts and my good friends, my cousins that I grew up with on that night.
Had I gone with my mother, both of us would have been dead also.
But she survived in Stuffhof, and she was liberated by the Russians.
And she eventually found me in Italy and died in Los Angeles 12 years ago.
We went to a camp which was a division of Dachau.
It was not really Dachau.
The actual camp was called Kaufering, Kaufering Eins.
There was Kaufering one through 10, 10 small camps.
We were administered by Dachau, but we were about 30, 40 miles away from Dachau itself.
It was near a small town called Landsberg, Landsberg am Lech, which is famous for having a prison in it where Hitler sat while he was writing And it's about 40 miles, 50 miles from Munchen, Munich.
It was a small camp, 3,000 inmates in it.
And there were 10 camps like that, 30,000-- all together, 30,000 slaves.
It was not a death camp.
What we did was build an aircraft factory.
The Germans were building an underground aircraft factory, which was the most massive installation you could possibly imagine.
They took a large mountain and hollowed it out and reinforced it with cement, and they dug a hole into the mountain about as deep as the mountain was high.
And on one side-- they had three railroad lines running into it.
And on one side, they already had machinery that was being installed.
On the other side, they were still excavating.
And on top of the mountain, it was a mountain.
Goats were running around.
Trees were growing, you know.
No bomber could possibly go through it.
Eventually, the Americans tried to blow the damn thing up after they got there, you know, after the war.
They couldn't blow it up.
